title: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
description: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "A Vindication of the Rights of Woman" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: "Colophon"
memes.description: "Colophon for the Standard Ebooks edition of A Vindication of the Rights of Woman."

memes.label: "Dedication: To M. Talleyrand Perigord"
memes.description: "Sir:—"

memes.label: "Endnotes"
memes.description: "Endnotes and references for A Vindication of the Rights of Woman."

memes.label: "Imprint"
memes.description: "Publishing imprint for the 1792 edition of A Vindication of the Rights of Woman."

memes.label: "Introduction"
memes.description: "After considering the historic page, and viewing the living world with anxious solicitude, the most melancholy emotions of sorrowful indignation have depressed…"

memes.label: "John Locke"
memes.description: "John Locke (1632–1704), English philosopher whose ideas on the mind and education are cited by Wollstonecraft in support of her argument that the understanding is formed through experience and association."

memes.label: Table of Contents
memes.description: Table of Contents for A Vindication of the Rights of Woman

memes.label: "Uncopyright"
memes.description: "Public domain dedication for A Vindication of the Rights of Woman."

memes.label: "A Discourse on Inequality"
memes.description: "Philosophical treatise by Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1755) on the origins of social inequality — the source of the 'state of nature' arguments that Wollstonecraft critiques in Chapter I."

memes.label: "A Father's Legacy to His Daughters"
memes.description: "Conduct book by Dr. John Gregory (1774) — posthumously published advice to his daughters that Wollstonecraft critiques for teaching dissimulation rather than virtue."

memes.label: "Adam Smith"
memes.description: "Scottish moral philosopher and economist (1723–1790) whose Theory of Moral Sentiments Wollstonecraft quotes at length to structure her critique of wealth, rank, and the condition of women."

memes.label: "Adèle et Théodore"
memes.description: "Educational novel by Madame de Genlis (1782) — a widely read work on female education that Wollstonecraft references in her survey of contemporary educational literature."

memes.label: "Alexander Pope"
memes.description: "English poet (1688–1744) — cited by Wollstonecraft in her discussion of women's character and the power of education to shape it."

memes.label: "An Essay on Man"
memes.description: "Philosophical poem by Alexander Pope (1733–1734) — cited by Wollstonecraft as an expression of Enlightenment optimism about human nature and the order of the universe."

memes.label: "Areopagitica"
memes.description: "Polemic by John Milton (1644) defending freedom of speech and the press — its arguments for intellectual liberty resonate with Wollstonecraft's case for women's intellectual emancipation."

memes.label: Ars Amatoria
memes.description: Ovid's didactic elegy on the art of seduction and love (c. 1 BCE–1 CE), a work from the classical literary tradition that forms part of the cultural backdrop to Wollstonecraft's discussion of gender and courtship in A Vindication of the Rights of Woman.

memes.label: "Beauty"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's critical examination of beauty as a social construct that reduces women to objects of desire — contrasted with 'mental beauty', the genuine attractiveness of a cultivated mind and virtuous character."

memes.label: "Benevolence"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft values benevolence as a genuine moral sentiment but warns that it must be guided by reason and justice — benevolence without principle degenerates into mere feeling, and the 'benevolence' that keeps women in tutelage is a form of tyranny."

memes.label: "Catharine Macaulay"
memes.description: "English historian and republican writer (1731–1791) — one of the few contemporary women Wollstonecraft praises unequivocally, cited as an example of what women can achieve when permitted a serious education."

memes.label: "Chapter I"
memes.description: "The rights and involved duties of mankind considered."

memes.label: "Chapter II"
memes.description: "The prevailing opinion of a sexual character discussed."

memes.label: "Chapter III"
memes.description: "The same subject continued."

memes.label: "Chapter IV"
memes.description: "Observations on the state of degradation to which woman is reduced by various causes."

memes.label: "Chapter V"
memes.description: "Animadversions on some of the writers who have rendered women objects of pity, bordering on contempt."

memes.label: "Chapter VI"
memes.description: "The effect which an early association of ideas has upon the character."

memes.label: "Chapter VII"
memes.description: "Modesty comprehensively considered and not as a sexual virtue."

memes.label: "Chapter VIII"
memes.description: "Morality undermined by sexual notions of the importance of a good reputation."

memes.label: "Chapter IX"
memes.description: "Of the pernicious effects which arise from the unnatural distinctions established in society."

memes.label: "Chapter X"
memes.description: "Parental affection."

memes.label: "Chapter XI"
memes.description: "Duty to parents."

memes.label: "Chapter XII"
memes.description: "On national education."

memes.label: "Chapter XIII"
memes.description: "Some instances of the folly which the ignorance of women generates; with concluding reflections on the moral improvement that a revolution in female manners…"

memes.label: "Character"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft argues that character is formed by education and social circumstance rather than innate disposition, and that the sexual character attributed to women is an artificial construct that degrades both sexes."

memes.label: "Charles Maurice de Talleyrand-Périgord"
memes.description: "French statesman and diplomat (1754–1838), dedicatee of the Vindication — Wollstonecraft addresses her argument to him as a legislator, challenging his 1791 report that proposed excluding women from public education."

memes.label: Chastity
memes.description: A virtue Wollstonecraft redefines from a sex-specific social enforcement mechanism into a universal moral quality grounded in reason rather than reputation.

memes.label: "Civilization"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's ambivalent assessment of civilization — critiqued as 'very partial' in its benefits yet defended against Rousseau's primitivism as the necessary condition for moral and intellectual progress."

memes.label: "Coeducation"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's radical proposal that boys and girls should be educated together in public schools — a foundational argument for gender equality in education that challenges the separate-spheres doctrine of eighteenth-century educational theory."

memes.label: Conduct Book
memes.description: A genre of instructional literature prescribing proper behaviour for women, extensively critiqued by Wollstonecraft in A Vindication of the Rights of Woman as contributing to the enfeebling of the female mind.

memes.label: Coquetry
memes.description: The art of attracting male attention through calculated charm and flirtation, which Wollstonecraft identifies as a rational response by women to a social system that denies them other forms of power, yet ultimately degrading to both sexes.

memes.label: "Cunning"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's analysis of cunning as 'the natural opponent of strength' — a psychological strategy adopted by the weak, particularly women denied access to legitimate power and rational education."

memes.label: "David Hume"
memes.description: "Scottish Enlightenment philosopher (1711–1776) whose gendered analysis of French versus Athenian society Wollstonecraft quotes in ch04 to illustrate the arbitrary power women hold over men."

memes.label: "Delicacy"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft critiques the cult of female delicacy — the exaggerated sensitivity and refinement demanded of women — as a form of artificial weakness that renders women unfit for genuine virtue and moral independence."

memes.label: "Despotism"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's analysis of despotism as a corrupting force that operates not only in the state but in the household — the arbitrary exercise of power that degrades both the oppressor and the oppressed."

memes.label: "Dissimulation"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's critique of dissimulation as a socially enforced practice of concealment and deceit that corrupts women's character by compelling them to suppress their true feelings and opinions under a system of artificial manners."

memes.label: "Dress"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's sustained critique of women's preoccupation with dress as a socially constructed diversion, not a natural female inclination — arising from the want of mental cultivation and the restricted sphere in which women are forced to operate."

memes.label: "Duty"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's conception of duty is grounded in reason rather than social convention — genuine duty flows from understanding one's moral obligations, and the duties imposed on women by society are often a perversion of true moral duty."

memes.label: "Education"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's argument that education — the cultivation of reason and virtue through individual development and social institutions — is the key to personal excellence and social progress, systematically denied to women."

memes.label: "Émile, or On Education"
memes.description: "Philosophical treatise on education by Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1762) — the primary target of Wollstonecraft's critique of gendered education, particularly Book V on the education of Sophie."

memes.label: "Equality"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's argument that social and moral equality between the sexes is the foundation of virtue, happiness, and a just society — contrasted with the artificial inequality produced by education, prejudice, and hereditary privilege."

memes.label: "Folly"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft uses 'folly' as a term of art for the moral and intellectual errors produced by a corrupt education — the follies of women are not natural but are the predictable consequences of a system that denies them reason and cultivates vanity instead."

memes.label: "Fortitude"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's conception of fortitude as a cardinal virtue that should be cultivated universally, regardless of sex; paired with truth as essential to moral character and contrasted with the false 'patience' imposed on women."

memes.label: "Francis Bacon"
memes.description: "English philosopher and statesman (1561–1626), referenced by Wollstonecraft as an exemplar of intellectual achievement and the empirical method."

memes.label: Freedom
memes.description: Wollstonecraft's conception of freedom as the capacity for rational self-governance, distinct from the lawless license that passes for liberty in an unjust social order.

memes.label: "Friendship"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft elevates friendship above romantic love as the ideal form of human relationship — a steady, rational affection grounded in mutual respect and shared virtue rather than transient passion or dependency."

memes.label: Gallantry
memes.description: A system of exaggerated male politeness and attentiveness toward women that Wollstonecraft identifies as a disguised form of despotism, masking women's subordination beneath a veneer of deference.

memes.label: "Genius"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's ambivalent treatment of genius — admired as the highest expression of human intellect, yet critiqued as a concept used to exclude women from intellectual life and to excuse the excesses of male sentiment."

memes.label: "Germaine de Stael"
memes.description: "French intellectual and author (1766–1817) whose enthusiastic praise of Rousseau's sensualism Wollstonecraft sharply critiques in ch05 as exemplifying the false consciousness of women who embrace their own subordination."

memes.label: "Grace"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft critiques the elevation of grace to a central feminine virtue — grace is a pleasing quality but it is not a moral quality, and an education that prioritizes grace over substance produces women who are ornamental rather than virtuous."

memes.label: "Habit"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's concept of habit as a formative force in moral psychology; the accumulated force of repeated actions that shapes character, for better or worse, and can override reason when unchecked."

memes.label: "Happiness"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft grounds happiness in the cultivation of reason and virtue rather than in pleasure or the satisfaction of desire — true happiness requires independence of mind and moral agency, which the social order denies to women."

memes.label: "Hester Thrale Piozzi"
memes.description: "British author and literary figure (1741–1821) whose conduct-book advice — that a woman's arts should be solely employed to 'gain and keep the heart of man' — Wollstonecraft sharply critiques in ch05 as 'true masculine sentiments.'"

memes.label: "Humanity"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft grounds her argument in a universal conception of humanity — all human beings share the same rational nature and therefore the same moral claims, making the denial of rights to women a violation of humanity itself."

memes.label: "Humility"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's philosophical distinction of humility from modesty; humility is framed as a Christian virtue that, when misapplied to women, becomes a tool of subjugation rather than genuine moral development."

memes.label: Ignorance
memes.description: In A Vindication of the Rights of Woman, ignorance — particularly the cultivated ignorance imposed on women through inadequate education — is identified as the root cause of female subordination and a barrier to virtue.

memes.label: Imagination
memes.description: A mental faculty that Wollstonecraft identifies as both a source of human excellence when governed by reason and a dangerous inflamer of sensibility when left unchecked — particularly in women's education.

memes.label: "Independence"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's ideal of independence as the foundation of character and virtue — encompassing intellectual self-reliance, economic autonomy, and moral dignity — and her argument that women are systematically denied the conditions for its development."

memes.label: "Innocence"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's critique of 'innocence' as a 'civil term for weakness' — the specious ideal used to justify keeping women in a state of ignorance and perpetual childhood rather than cultivating their reason and virtue."

memes.label: "James Fordyce"
memes.description: "Scottish Presbyterian clergyman and poet (1720–1796), author of Sermons to Young Women — a conduct book Wollstonecraft singles out for particular scorn as the epitome of the literature that infantilises women under the guise of moral instruction."

memes.label: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
memes.description: Genevan philosopher and writer whose views on women and education, particularly in Emile, are extensively critiqued in A Vindication of the Rights of Woman.

memes.label: "Dr. John Gregory"
memes.description: "Scottish physician and moralist (1724–1773), author of A Father's Legacy to His Daughters — one of the conduct-book writers Wollstonecraft critiques for training women in dissimulation rather than virtue."

relations.label: A Father's Legacy to His Daughters

memes.label: John Locke
memes.description: English philosopher and physician whose views on education and the development of reason are approvingly cited in A Vindication of the Rights of Woman.

memes.label: "John Milton"
memes.description: "English poet (1608–1674), author of Paradise Lost — cited by Wollstonecraft in her discussion of Eve, prelapsarian virtue, and the nature of the relationship between the sexes before the Fall."

memes.label: "Judgment"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's conception of judgment as a cultivated rational faculty — the 'foundation of all taste' and a capacity systematically denied to women through an education that substitutes prejudice for independent thought."

memes.label: "Justice"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft demands justice — not charity or gallantry — for women, grounding her claim in universal moral law and the rational capacities shared by both sexes. The failure to grant women justice is a failure of the entire social order."

memes.label: "Knowledge"
memes.description: "For Wollstonecraft, knowledge flows naturally from the exercise of reason and is essential to virtue; the denial of knowledge to women is a central injustice, keeping them dependent, frivolous, and incapable of fulfilling their moral duties."

memes.label: "Letters on Education"
memes.description: "Educational treatise by Catharine Macaulay (1790) — one of the few contemporary works on female education that Wollstonecraft cites approvingly, arguing for co-education and the cultivation of reason in both sexes."

memes.label: "Letters to His Son"
memes.description: "Epistolary work by Lord Chesterfield (1774) — a manual of worldly wisdom that Wollstonecraft critiques as reducing morality to polished manners and social performance."

memes.label: "Liberty"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's conception of liberty as both a political condition and a state of mind — the freedom from arbitrary power and from the internalised constraints of a degrading education, which she argues is necessary for the cultivation of virtue."

memes.label: "Lord Chesterfield"
memes.description: "Philip Dormer Stanhope, 4th Earl of Chesterfield (1694–1773), author of Letters to His Son — cited by Wollstonecraft as an example of the worldly, cynical education that produces polished manners at the expense of genuine virtue."

memes.label: "Louis XIV"
memes.description: "King of France (1638–1715) whose court culture of 'factitious manners' and despotic gallantry Wollstonecraft uses as the paradigmatic example of how absolute power corrupts relations between the sexes."

memes.label: "Love"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's analysis of love is famously unsentimental — she argues that romantic love is necessarily transient and that a social order built on women's capacity to inspire and sustain it condemns them to eventual irrelevance and misery."

memes.label: "Luxury"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's critique of luxury as a corrupting force that enfeebles both mind and body; paired with superstition as one of the twin enemies of reason and virtue in her moral psychology."

memes.label: Madame de Genlis
memes.description: French writer and educator whose children's books and works on education are briefly noted by Wollstonecraft as useful but limited by narrow views and unreasonable prejudices.

memes.label: "Madame de Genlis"
memes.description: "Stéphanie Félicité du Crest de Saint-Aubin, Comtesse de Genlis (1746–1830), French educational writer — referenced by Wollstonecraft as a contemporary female author whose works on education merit attention."

memes.label: "Manners"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft distinguishes between genuine morality and mere manners — the outward forms of propriety that society demands of women but that have no necessary connection to virtue. The confusion of manners with morals is a central target of her critique."

memes.label: "Marriage"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's critique of marriage as a social institution that, in its 18th-century form, reduced women to economic dependents and undermined their capacity for virtue and reason."

memes.label: "Mesmerism"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's extended critique of mesmerism and animal magnetism as fashionable deceptions that prey on women's credulity — a major 1780s-90s controversy she engages in Section 13.1 as a case study of the folly produced by female ignorance."

memes.label: "Metamorphoses"
memes.description: "Narrative poem by Ovid (8 CE) — the classical mythological compendium that provided the shared cultural vocabulary for eighteenth-century discussions of love and transformation."

memes.label: "Modesty"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft distinguishes true modesty — a sober, rational self-assessment — from the performative sexual modesty imposed on women as a 'virtue' that serves male interests while degrading female character."

memes.label: "Moral Essays"
memes.description: "Poetic work by Alexander Pope (1731–1735), particularly the Epistle to a Lady: Of the Characters of Women — cited by Wollstonecraft as the canonical expression of the view of female character she contests."

memes.label: "Morality"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft argues that morality must be grounded in reason rather than sentiment or convention — true morality is the same for both sexes, and the spurious morality of reputation and obedience corrupts rather than elevates."

memes.label: "National Education"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's core reform proposal for state-funded, coeducational day schools open to all classes, articulated in Chapter XII as the foundation for creating enlightened citizens and virtuous women."

memes.label: "Nature"
memes.description: "A complex concept in Wollstonecraft's argument — sometimes invoked as an idealized state from which society has fallen, more often critiqued as a rhetorical tool used to justify women's subordination through appeals to supposed natural differences."

memes.label: "Novum Organum"
memes.description: "Philosophical work by Francis Bacon (1620) advocating empirical scientific method — cited by Wollstonecraft as a landmark of the intellectual achievement made possible by education."

memes.label: "Obedience"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's critique of obedience as a demanded female virtue — the expectation that women should submit without question to male authority, which she argues is incompatible with rational agency and genuine moral development."

memes.label: "Oppression"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft identifies oppression — not natural inferiority — as the cause of women's degraded condition, arguing that the vices attributed to women are in fact 'produced by oppression' rather than inherent to the female character."

memes.label: "Ovid"
memes.description: "Roman poet (43 BCE–17 CE), author of the Metamorphoses — referenced by Wollstonecraft as a classical literary authority."

memes.label: "Parental Affection"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's theory of parental affection as a cultivated moral sentiment rather than an innate instinct — critiquing the blind, tyrannical love that sacrifices justice and reason to narrow family interests."

memes.label: "Passion"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's treatment of the passions is complex — she acknowledges their divine implantation and role in unfolding reason, but critiques their unregulated indulgence, particularly the sexual passions that, in her analysis, degrade women when made the basis of their social value."

memes.label: "Power"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's dual analysis of power — arbitrary power as a corrupting force that degrades both wielder and subject, contrasted with legitimate authority grounded in reason and virtue."

memes.label: "Prejudice"
memes.description: "A central critical concept in Wollstonecraft's epistemology — prejudices are inherited opinions that substitute for genuine understanding, cloud reason, and perpetuate the subordination of women under the guise of natural order."

memes.label: "Property"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's radical critique of property as the root of social corruption; the respect paid to property, rather than to virtue or talent, constitutes the primary source of moral and political evil."

memes.label: "Prudence"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft critiques 'worldly prudence' as a degraded substitute for genuine virtue — a calculating self-interest that women are taught to cultivate in lieu of moral reasoning, reducing ethics to the management of appearances."

memes.label: "Rank"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft critiques the corrupting influence of rank — both the hereditary aristocracy and the gendered hierarchy — arguing that rank substitutes external position for internal merit and degrades the character of both the powerful and the subordinate."

memes.label: "Rapport sur l'instruction publique"
memes.description: "Report to the French National Assembly by Charles Maurice de Talleyrand-Périgord (1791) proposing a system of national education that excluded girls — the immediate political catalyst for Wollstonecraft's Vindication."

memes.label: "Reason"
memes.description: "The faculty of rational thought that Wollstonecraft posits as the foundation of virtue, knowledge, and human perfection — applicable equally to both sexes and the distinguishing mark of humanity."

memes.label: "Refinement"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's critique of 'false refinement' — the artificial cultivation of sensibility, taste, and manners that she argues weakens women's character and substitutes superficial elegance for genuine virtue."

memes.label: "Reform"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft calls for a radical reformation of manners, education, and the social position of women — not piecemeal improvement but a fundamental restructuring of the relations between the sexes grounded in reason."

memes.label: Religion
memes.description: Wollstonecraft's argument that genuine religious faith requires the exercise of reason, and that women's exclusion from intellectual development undermines their capacity for authentic religious devotion.

memes.label: "Reputation"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft devotes an entire chapter to exposing reputation as a hollow substitute for virtue — women are taught to guard their reputation rather than cultivate their character, valuing appearance over moral substance."

memes.label: "Respect"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft argues that genuine respect must be earned through moral conduct and reason, not demanded by social position or custom — the key distinction being between respect that flows from virtue and the hollow deference demanded by patriarchal hierarchy."

memes.label: "Rights"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's argument that women, as rational beings, are entitled to the same natural and civil rights as men — and that denying these rights undermines the moral foundation of society itself."

memes.label: "Rights of Woman"
memes.description: "The central claim of Wollstonecraft's Vindication — that women, as rational beings, possess the same natural rights as men, and that the denial of these rights is the root cause of women's degradation and the corruption of society."

memes.label: "Jean-Jacques Rousseau"
memes.description: "Genevan philosopher (1712–1778), author of Émile and The Social Contract — the primary intellectual antagonist of the Vindication, whose gendered educational theory Wollstonecraft systematically dismantles."

memes.label: "Sensibility"
memes.description: "A key term in eighteenth-century moral philosophy that Wollstonecraft critiques as a degraded substitute for reason — the cultivation of refined feeling that, when made the foundation of female virtue, produces weakness rather than moral strength."

memes.label: "Sentiment"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft engages critically with the eighteenth-century cult of sentiment — she values genuine feeling but insists it must be governed by reason, warning that untutored sentiment leads women into moral error and makes them vulnerable to manipulation."

memes.label: "Sentimental Novel"
memes.description: "Wollstonecrafts critique of sentimental novels as a genre that corrupts womens minds by substituting romantic fantasy for rational understanding, inflaming the imagination at the expense of the understanding, and distracting women from the genuine duties of life."

memes.label: "Sermons to Young Women"
memes.description: "Conduct book by James Fordyce (1766) — dismissed by Wollstonecraft as florid and sentimental, an example of the literature that infantilises women under the guise of moral instruction."

memes.label: "Sophia"
memes.description: "Rousseau's fictional ideal woman from Book V of Emile, whose education in docility, cunning, and pleasing is the central target of Wollstonecraft's critique of sex-differentiated education."

memes.label: Strength
memes.description: In A Vindication of the Rights of Woman, strength — both of body and mind — is a foundational requirement for virtue, independence, and the proper exercise of reason.

memes.label: "Taste"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's critical treatment of 'taste' as an aesthetic value that, when elevated above moral and intellectual development, becomes a symptom of weakness — 'refinement of taste' is classed among the 'epithets of weakness' that degrade women."

memes.label: The Advancement of Learning
memes.description: Francis Bacon's 1605 treatise on the classification and advancement of knowledge, a foundational text of the empirical tradition that Wollstonecraft draws upon in her argument for women's intellectual equality.

memes.label: "The History of England"
memes.description: "Major historical work by Catharine Macaulay (1763–1783), an eight-volume Whig history — cited by Wollstonecraft as evidence of what women can achieve intellectually when given a serious education."

memes.label: "The Social Contract"
memes.description: "Political treatise by Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1762) — referenced by Wollstonecraft in discussions of political obligation, rights, and the nature of legitimate authority."

memes.label: Théâtre d'Éducation
memes.description: A collection of educational plays for children by Madame de Genlis, published in 1779–1780, reflecting the moral-didactic approach to female education that Wollstonecraft critiques in A Vindication of the Rights of Woman.

memes.label: "Truth"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's conception of truth as a simple, universal principle accessible through reason, standing in opposition to sentiment, prejudice, and artificial social conventions."

memes.label: "Tyranny"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's analysis of tyranny as arbitrary domination — operating in the domestic sphere as much as the political, through the 'iron sceptre' of unchecked power over wives, children, and dependents."

memes.label: "Understanding"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft uses 'understanding' as a comprehensive term for the rational faculty — including reason, judgment, and intellectual capacity — and argues that women's understanding, when properly cultivated through education, is equal to men's."

memes.label: "Vanity"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft identifies vanity as one of the principal vices produced by women's degraded education — when women are taught to value only their appearance and the admiration of others, vanity becomes a substitute for genuine self-worth grounded in virtue and reason."

memes.label: "Virtue"
memes.description: "Central moral concept in Wollstonecraft's philosophy — the cultivation of reason and moral excellence as the foundation of individual character and social progress, applicable equally to both sexes."

memes.label: "Wealth"
memes.description: "Wollstonecraft's critique of hereditary wealth as a corrupting force that undermines virtue, distorts social relations, and perpetuates inequality by substituting the accident of birth for merit."

memes.label: "Wisdom"
memes.description: "Wisdom in Wollstonecraft's framework is the mature fruit of reason and experience — the capacity to judge rightly in practical matters and to direct one's life according to rational principles rather than impulse or convention."
